name: Uncomplicated UTI (Women)

key_questions: Classic triad: dysuria + frequency + urgency (without fever/flank pain — lower tract only)?
No structural urinary abnormality, immunosuppression, or renal disease?
Previous UTIs and response to nitrofurantoin?
Vaginal discharge or irritation (may suggest vaginitis vs UTI)?

Ontario MA UTI criteria: non-pregnant female aged 16–64, no fever/flank pain/systemic symptoms (i.e., no upper UTI), no recurrent UTI (≥2 in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months), no antibiotics for UTI in past 3 months, no urinary catheter, no known urinary structural/functional abnormality
Pregnancy test recommended before initiating treatment (Ontario MA requirement)
Document and notify primary care provider of MA prescribing (Ontario MA requirement)
red_flags: Fever ≥38°C, rigors, flank/CVA tenderness, nausea/vomiting → PYELONEPHRITIS — not minor ailment, needs full assessment
Pregnancy → complicated UTI — refer for culture + appropriate antibiotic
Male, elderly, immunocompromised, recurrent (>3/year) → complicated UTI — not minor ailment
Hematuria alone without classic UTI symptoms → other pathology (stones, cancer) — investigate

first_line.name: Nitrofurantoin Macrobid
first_line.drugs: Nitrofurantoin macrocrystals (Macrobid) 100 mg BID × 5 days WITH food
first_line.notes: First-line for uncomplicated UTI in Ontario (Bugs & Drugs — low E. coli resistance <10%). Take with food — improves absorption and reduces GI side effects. Only for lower tract UTI — NOT for pyelonephritis (inadequate tissue penetration). Avoid if eGFR <30. Urine may turn brown/yellow — harmless. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) are EXCLUDED from Ontario MA UTI algorithm (Health Canada/FDA black box; reserve for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis under physician care).
first_line.preferred_when: First-line uncomplicated UTI, non-pregnant women, eGFR ≥30

second_line.name: TMP-SMX DS
second_line.notes: Use only if local E. coli resistance <20% (check Bugs & Drugs Ontario). Note: TMP-SMX should not be used empirically if local E. coli resistance >20%, which now exceeds this threshold in many Ontario regions — verify local antibiogram before use. Sulfonamide allergy contraindication. Drug interactions: hyperkalemia with ACEi/ARBs (monitor K+), INR elevation with warfarin. Avoid in last trimester of pregnancy, G6PD deficiency, and sulfa allergy.

second_line.name: Fosfomycin
second_line.drugs: Fosfomycin 3g granules single oral dose (dissolve in water)
second_line.notes: Single-dose — excellent adherence. Effective for uncomplicated UTI. ODB coverage limited. Take on empty stomach.

second_line.name: Beta-Lactam Alternatives (when first-line CI'd)
second_line.drugs: Cephalexin 500 mg QID × 3–7 days | Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID × 3–7 days (less preferred due to resistance)
second_line.notes: Use when nitrofurantoin and TMP-SMX are contraindicated. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is less preferred due to higher resistance rates and broader spectrum impact.

non_pharm: Increase fluid intake (dilute urine reduces bacterial concentration)
Avoid spermicides and diaphragm (alter vaginal flora)

Phenazopyridine (pyridium) 95 mg tablets — OTC dose: 190 mg (2 tablets) TID × maximum 2 days: bladder analgesic — relieves burning/urgency while antibiotics work; turns urine orange (warn patient); NOT an antibiotic. Note: 200 mg TID is US Rx strength — Canadian OTC tablet is 95 mg.
D-mannose: Evidence controversial; MERIT trial 2024 (JAMA) showed no benefit over placebo for recurrence prevention. Low-quality evidence only.
refer_when: Pregnancy (complicated UTI — culture + appropriate antibiotic required)
Recurrent UTI (≥2 in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months)
Symptoms of upper UTI: fever >38°C, flank pain, rigors, nausea/vomiting (pyelonephritis)
Antibiotic use for UTI in prior 3 months
Hematuria without classic UTI symptoms (stones, cancer — investigate)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infection. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
AMMI Canada. Community-acquired Urinary Tract Infections in Adults — Canadian Guidelines (2019).

Gupta K, et al. International Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Treatment of Acute Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis in Women: 2010 Update by IDSA and ESCMID. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103–e120. (with AMMI Canada local antibiogram updates).
NICE. Urinary tract infection (lower) — women: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
